topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s pronouncements about the sprawling, slightly dilapidated Victorian house were tedious. I stood by the dusty bay window, letting the afternoon sun warm the back of my neck, and watched the dust motes dance in the light. My brother, Mark, was already rummaging through the attic, a loud, energetic flurry of boxes and forgotten trinkets. I traced a pattern on the condensation clinging to the glass. The inheritance felt like a heavy, unnecessary weight settling onto my shoulders. I didn't feel a surge of excitement, nor did I feel any particular dread; just a flat, unremarkable acceptance of the situation. "Well," I murmured, the sound barely disturbing the silence, "this is what we get."

[story 2]

I surveyed the drawing-room with a detached gaze. The wallpaper was peeling in long, curling strips, and the furniture sagged with the weight of decades. My hands rested loosely at my sides, my posture relaxed to the point of near collapse against the worn velvet of a nearby armchair. Mark was attempting to fix a wobbly leg on a mahogany table, his brow furrowed in concentration. I observed him for a moment, noting the sheer effort he was expending, and then turned my attention to the window overlooking the overgrown garden. The air smelled of damp earth and old roses. A faint sigh escaped me, more of a release of held breath than actual emotion, as I considered the sheer volume of tasks ahead. It was simply… there.

[story 3]

The keys felt cold and surprisingly heavy in my palm. I walked through the main hall, my footsteps echoing hollowly on the parquet floor. Everything felt muted, as if the world outside this threshold had been deliberately dimmed. I stopped before a portrait of my grandmother, a woman whose expression seemed perfectly composed, utterly unreadable. I didn't feel compelled to touch the frame or examine the faded fabric of her dress. My gaze drifted over the high ceilings, cataloging the decay—the water stains, the sagging plaster—with the precise scrutiny one might apply to a poorly maintained inventory. "Right," I said, the word flat and even, not quite a statement, just an acknowledgment of the reality settling in.
